ideas.name: Grant Compliance Assistant
ideas.one_liner: Answers compliance and reporting questions — funding eligibility, expenditure rules, reporting deadlines — by searching across an institution's uploaded grant agreements, donor policies, and budget circulars.
ideas.problem: Finance teams at PHINMA, Rising Academies, and Acasus-supported institutions manage funding from multiple sources with conflicting conditions: government grants with restricted expenditure categories, World Bank loans with procurement requirements, philanthropic grants with earmarking rules. The answers to compliance questions are buried in lengthy PDF agreements. Knowledge is concentrated in one or two staff members who become bottlenecks and single points of failure.
ideas.ai_technique: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (grant-document-aware)
ideas.how_it_works: Upload all institutional funding documents — grant agreements, donor policy manuals, government funding circulars, audit requirement schedules — to a vector store. When a finance officer asks 'Can we use the CHED grant for staff overtime?' or 'What is the reporting deadline for the AfDB disbursement?', retrieve the relevant clauses and generate a cited answer with document reference and page number. Flag ambiguous clauses that require legal or donor clarification. Output a searchable compliance Q&A log and a summary of all active reporting deadlines by funder.
ideas.scoring_rationale: Near-perfect profile for a RAG product: documents exist and are uploadable, the buyer (CFO or Grants Manager) has clear authority and budget, and ROI is visible within days of deployment. Closest to GrantLens of any batch-2 idea — functionally this is a core GrantLens feature, not a standalone product.

ideas.name: Teacher Attrition Forecaster
ideas.one_liner: Predicts teacher attrition by cohort and subject area over the next 2-3 academic terms, using contract milestones, certification expiry dates, and seasonal departure patterns to produce a forward hiring plan.
ideas.problem: Rising Academies and PHINMA face persistent teacher retention pressure, with attrition concentrated at predictable points — end-of-year contract decisions, certification renewal deadlines, and post-exam-season departures. HR teams discover vacancies too late to recruit without disrupting classes, and legacy HRIS cannot produce a forward-looking staffing gap analysis by subject area and campus.
ideas.ai_technique: Time Series Forecasting (school-calendar-aware, contract-aware)
ideas.how_it_works: Ingest teacher roster records: contract type, subject area, tenure, certification expiry, school assignment, and historical departure dates. Model attrition as a combination of seasonal patterns (end-of-year peaks) and event-driven signals (contracts expiring within 90 days, certifications lapsing). Forecast hiring demand for the next 2-3 terms by subject and campus, with confidence intervals. Output a monthly hiring plan, individual-level risk alerts for teachers with imminent contract or certification triggers, and recommended recruitment lead-time by subject scarcity.
ideas.buyer_persona: Chief People Officer or Talent Manager
ideas.scoring_rationale: Teacher attrition is a severe operational problem in sub-Saharan Africa and SE Asian affordable HE — a missed hire can mean a class with no teacher for weeks. Data is accessible from contract and registration records, and a working v1 model can be validated within one academic term. Duplicate of idea 3 but with stronger emphasis on forward hiring planning.

ideas.name: Fee Default Risk Predictor
ideas.one_liner: Scores enrolled students monthly on tuition default risk using payment history, course performance, and financial aid status, giving student services teams a prioritized intervention list before students stop attending.
ideas.problem: PHINMA and iPeople serve first-generation, lower-income students for whom fee default is the primary path to dropout. Institutions currently discover default only when a student stops attending — by which point re-engagement is difficult. Earlier identification (4-6 weeks before default) would allow targeted interventions: payment plan adjustment, emergency bursary, or scholarship extension. No existing SIS module provides this forward-looking signal.
ideas.ai_technique: Propensity & Behavioral Modeling (education-constrained)
ideas.how_it_works: Build a monthly default risk model on enrolled students using: prior fee payment timing patterns, current academic standing, changes in enrolment load (partial withdrawal signals), financial aid type and remaining balance, and family income proxy. Score each student and segment into intervention tiers — high-risk students get flagged for proactive contact by student services within the same week. Unlike credit risk models, the output is a support referral, not a service cutoff. Include a recommended intervention type (payment plan, emergency aid, scholarship review) based on the student's financial profile.
ideas.buyer_persona: Chief Student Services Officer or Registrar
ideas.scoring_rationale: Strong problem severity — fee default leading to dropout is a daily operational crisis in affordable HE. Data exists in SIS and financial records, and a working model can be validated against one term's cohort within the first deployment cycle. Overlaps with idea 44 and partially with idea 28.

ideas.name: Strategic Intelligence Digest
ideas.one_liner: Monitors education policy releases, donor priority updates, and sector research publications weekly, then delivers a 2-page synthesised briefing to leadership — replacing hours of manual document review.
ideas.problem: Acasus and WISE depend on staying ahead of policy changes, donor priority shifts, and sector evidence to inform strategy and win proposals. Leadership teams cannot maintain systematic coverage of government white papers, World Bank reports, UNESCO assessments, and sector publications — the volume is too high and the signal-to-noise ratio is low. Manual synthesis is done ad-hoc when someone happens to read the right document. Organisations risk building strategies on stale or incomplete intelligence.
ideas.ai_technique: Multi-document abstractive summarization + semantic topic clustering
ideas.how_it_works: A monitoring pipeline watches configured sources: government education department feeds, international development agency publication pages, donor priority statements from USAID, FCDO, and Gates Foundation, and sector publication RSS feeds. New documents are batch-processed weekly. The pipeline clusters content by theme, surfaces contradictions between sources — two reports drawing opposite conclusions on blended learning impact in LMIC contexts — and generates a 2-page briefing with hyperlinked citations. Leadership receives the briefing each Monday morning, reviews it in 10 minutes, and can request deep-dives on any flagged theme.
ideas.scoring_rationale: Strong across every dimension: publicly accessible data, fast build, clear executive buyer, and tangible weekly time saving from day one. The multi-step pipeline — monitor, cluster, synthesize, surface contradictions — genuinely benefits from agentic orchestration rather than a single prompt.

ideas.name: Teacher Risk Intervention Queue
ideas.one_liner: Ranks teachers monthly on three risk dimensions — flight risk, performance risk, and certification lapse — so HR can intervene before a departure, a class quality problem, or a compliance breach occurs.
ideas.problem: PHINMA's distributed workforce of 1,000+ teachers means HR cannot monitor individual risk manually. High performers in remote postings leave without warning. Teachers with expiring TESDA or PRC certifications continue in role past compliance deadlines. Staff in hard-to-fill subject areas resign without triggering a retention response. These are discovered only after the fact.
ideas.ai_technique: Risk Scoring & Assessment (education-specific factors)
ideas.how_it_works: Build a composite risk model across three dimensions: flight risk (tenure, remote posting, role-market wage gap, prior cohort departure patterns), performance risk (appraisal scores, student outcome trends, absence frequency), and compliance risk (certification expiry, registration renewal status). Score each teacher monthly and surface the top-risk cohort as a prioritized intervention queue. Each entry includes the dominant risk factor and a recommended action — targeted retention conversation, role adjustment, PD assignment, or urgent compliance follow-up.
ideas.buyer_persona: Head of HR or Talent Development Manager
ideas.scoring_rationale: Multi-dimensional risk scoring with intervention routing is a strong agentic use case — decomposing flight, performance, and compliance risk requires orchestrated data retrieval and reasoning, not just a lookup. Buyer clarity and problem severity are high; the main constraint is assembling clean input data across fragmented HR systems.

ideas.name: Capital Project Risk Monitor
ideas.one_liner: Monitors active capital projects for cost overrun trajectory, schedule slip, and funder compliance breaches, issuing early warnings before thresholds trigger funder sanctions.
ideas.problem: Acasus-supported institutions and Rising Academies run concurrent capital projects with tight funder timelines and fixed budgets. Cost overruns and schedule slippage are common but are typically identified only at monthly reporting — too late for low-cost remediation. Procurement rule breaches (e.g., equipment purchased outside approved thresholds) can trigger funder sanctions or full claw-back of disbursed funds.
ideas.ai_technique: Time Series Forecasting + Risk Scoring (project-specific)
ideas.how_it_works: Ingest capital project records: approved budget by category, spend-to-date, committed purchase orders, schedule baseline, milestone dates, and funder procurement rules. Track cost burn rate and schedule completion percentage against plan. Flag early warning signals when: spend trajectory is on course to exceed budget by >10% by project end; schedule completion is running >4 weeks behind baseline; procurement activities approach or exceed funder approval thresholds. Score each project's overall compliance risk on a weekly basis. Output a project health dashboard with traffic-light indicators, recommended remediation actions, and draft funder notification text for critical findings.
ideas.buyer_persona: Chief Operations Officer or Project Director
ideas.scoring_rationale: Funder claw-back risk makes cost overrun and compliance breaches acutely painful — this problem has real financial consequence, not just operational inconvenience. Agentic suitability is solid: the monitor needs to decompose budget trajectory, schedule, and compliance signals across multiple projects and generate differentiated alerts. Data access scores lower because project financial data is often in ERP systems requiring integration effort.

ideas.name: Regulatory Compliance Status Tracker
ideas.one_liner: Connects to NESA, QTSB, and CHED registration APIs and the in-house LMS to maintain a live compliance dashboard — flagging expired registrations and mandatory training gaps before they become liability events.
ideas.problem: Schools at CSPD and Rising Academies carry legal exposure every day a teacher works with an expired registration or overdue child safety clearance. HR teams track compliance in spreadsheets that fall out of sync as teachers complete training through different channels. A compliance gap discovered during an audit — or after an incident — creates serious regulatory and reputational consequences.
ideas.ai_technique: Data Transformation & Structuring with compliance schema and alert logic
ideas.how_it_works: Makes API calls to state registration authorities (NESA, QTSB, CHED) on a weekly schedule and compares returned status against the active staff list in TechOne HRIS. Pulls mandatory training completion records from the LMS (child safety, CPR, curriculum updates). Maintains a live compliance matrix per teacher: registration status, expiry date, training completion, and days to next renewal. Generates three outputs: a dashboard for HR (overall compliance %, names of non-compliant staff), automated email reminders to affected teachers at 60, 30, and 7 days before expiry, and an audit-ready compliance report in PDF.
ideas.buyer_persona: Compliance Officer or Head of HR responsible for regulatory adherence
ideas.scoring_rationale: High severity — an unregistered teacher on payroll is a liability event, not a minor inconvenience. Registration APIs exist and are callable. Time to value is fast: a working compliance dashboard can be deployed in under 8 weeks. The weak spot is defensibility — the core logic is rule-based alerting, not deep AI — but domain specificity (knowing which APIs to call and which frameworks to map to) creates real switching costs.

ideas.name: Enrollment Funnel Forecaster
ideas.one_liner: Forecasts cohort enrollment by program and term using 2-3 years of historical admission data, accounting for exam release dates, government scholarship timelines, and program-specific dropout rates.
ideas.problem: Enrollment managers at multi-campus HE operators like iPeople and PHINMA use spreadsheets and basic CRM reports to forecast intake. These tools miss education-sector patterns — government scholarship announcement timing, exam result release dates, and campus-specific conversion rates — meaning recruitment spend gets locked in before realistic projections are available.
ideas.ai_technique: Time Series Forecasting (academic-cycle-aware)
ideas.how_it_works: Ingest 2-3 years of enrollment history by program, term, and funnel stage (inquiry, application, enrollment, retention). Apply seasonal decomposition anchored to education-specific calendar events: exam dates, government funding announcements, school holidays. Output a term-by-program enrollment forecast with confidence intervals, giving enrollment managers a 12-week lead time to adjust recruitment spend and tune program capacity before each intake opens.
ideas.scoring_rationale: Enrollment forecasting is a genuine daily pain for HE operators, with clear buyers and accessible historical data. Scores are tempered by functional overlap with ideas 6, 41, and 73, and limited agentic depth — this is primarily a forecasting model, not an orchestrated pipeline.

ideas.name: Regulatory Change Impact Assessor
ideas.one_liner: Monitors CHED circulars, DepEd memoranda, and accreditor updates, then maps each new requirement against the institution's current compliance posture and flags gaps with a deadline-prioritised action list.
ideas.problem: iPeople, Kinetic, and PHINMA operate under continuous regulatory change — CHED curriculum standards, DepEd qualification requirements, NESA announcements — with compliance deadlines that can require 6-12 months of preparation. Policy signals are buried in government portal releases that no-one systematically monitors. Organisations discover a new requirement only when a peer announces it or an auditor raises it, leaving insufficient time to respond. Early movers who build monitoring capacity gain competitive advantage.
ideas.ai_technique: Entity extraction + impact logic modeling
ideas.how_it_works: A monitoring agent watches configured government and accreditor publication feeds daily. When new regulatory content is detected, named entity extraction pulls out specific rules, deadlines, affected qualification levels, and infrastructure requirements. Each extracted requirement is matched against the institution's current compliance profile — program catalogue, infrastructure inventory, staffing ratios — to classify it as already-compliant, gap-with-buffer, or at-risk. The output is a weekly regulatory digest plus an impact brief for each material change: 'New DepEd curriculum requirements add 120 contact hours per program; current allocation provides a 30-hour buffer. A curriculum audit is recommended before the September deadline.'
ideas.buyer_persona: Chief Academic Officer / VP of Compliance
ideas.scoring_rationale: Regulatory monitoring is a persistent daily burden with clear compliance consequences, and the gap-mapping step — comparing new requirements against the institution's current posture — is where the genuine AI value sits. Multi-step pipeline (monitor, extract, match, brief) is a strong fit for agentic orchestration.

ideas.name: Enrollment Intent Classifier
ideas.one_liner: Reads inbound SMS, WhatsApp, and email inquiries from prospective families and classifies them by enrollment intent and dropout risk — so admissions staff call the right people first.
ideas.problem: Admissions teams at Rising Academies and iPeople receive hundreds of weekly inquiries across SMS, WhatsApp, and email in markets where conversion rates sit at 5–15%. Staff manually read every message to decide who to call — missing cost-sensitive families who need a scholarship nudge and flagging high-intent families too late. Early dropout signals ('can't afford fees', 'might relocate') embedded in inquiry text go undetected until the student leaves.
ideas.ai_technique: Text Classification with education-specific intent taxonomy
ideas.how_it_works: Connects to WhatsApp Business API, email inbox, and web form submissions. Classifies each message into: high-intent enrollment, scholarship inquiry, fee-payment concern, program comparison, or dropout-risk. For dropout-risk flags, extracts the underlying signal (financial, geographic, academic) and routes to a student success officer with a suggested response. High-intent leads go to an admissions rep within the hour. Weekly digest shows funnel conversion by intent category, helping teams measure which outreach scripts work.
ideas.buyer_persona: Enrollment Manager or Student Success Officer at multi-campus or international institution
ideas.scoring_rationale: High immediate ROI — even a 2-percentage-point lift in conversion in a 10,000-inquiry market covers the product cost many times over. Time to value is fast: the classifier can be trained on historical WhatsApp exports and deployed in weeks. Duplicate risk is significant: ideas 17, 39, 72 in the full list all describe the same core concept.

ideas.name: Student Propensity Engine
ideas.one_liner: Scores each applicant's likelihood to enroll using structured admission data — GPA, family income proxy, prior school type, distance to campus — so admissions teams can prioritize outreach and avoid over-recruiting thin programs.
ideas.problem: PHINMA and iPeople process thousands of applications per intake cycle. Without a systematic propensity model, admissions staff apply the same outreach intensity to everyone, resulting in over-recruited programs and unfilled seats in others. Rising Academies in West Africa need the same logic applied to household demographic data to target community outreach before formal applications exist.
ideas.ai_technique: Structured Data Classification + Propensity Scoring
ideas.how_it_works: Train a classifier on historical applicant records — GPA, family income indicator, prior school type, campus distance, program choice, test score — against actual enrollment outcome. Score new applicants on enrollment propensity and segment into quintiles. High-propensity applicants get retention-focused outreach; low-propensity get intensive engagement. For Rising Academies, apply the model to neighborhood-level demographic data to generate a household enrollment likelihood map before outreach campaigns begin.
ideas.buyer_persona: Admissions Manager or Enrollment Strategy Lead
ideas.scoring_rationale: Clear buyer pain and a defined modeling approach, but functionally overlaps with ideas 7, 19, and 74 in the same batch and full list. Data access scores slightly lower because household demographic data for the Rising Academies use case requires non-trivial sourcing outside standard SIS exports.

ideas.problem: Finance teams at PHINMA and Rising Academies produce detailed variance reports monthly — actuals vs. budget across 20-50 cost centers and multiple funding sources. ideas.ai_technique: Summarization & Condensation (variance-specific)
ideas.how_it_works: Ingest monthly budget-vs-actual data by program, department, and funding source (CSV or ERP export). For each material variance, generate a plain-language explanation drawing on prior month narrative and known calendar context (e.g., delayed grant disbursement, enrollment shortfall, deferred capex). Output a one-page executive summary with the top five variances by impact, their explanations, and any required board or funder notification.
ideas.scoring_rationale: Very fast to build and fast to show value — CSV in, narrative out. But problem severity is moderate (finance teams find this tedious, not crisis-level) and defensibility is low because summarizing tabular data is a thin moat. Overlaps significantly with ideas 27 and 37.

ideas.name: Dropout Risk Forecaster
ideas.one_liner: Scores enrolled students by dropout probability at key moments — post-admission, mid-semester, pre-renewal — using payment history, LMS activity, and demographic signals, then surfaces a prioritised intervention list.
ideas.problem: iPeople and Rising Academies operate in price-sensitive segments where family financial stress and program mismatch are the primary dropout drivers. Advisors relying on single-factor flags — one missed payment, one absent week — identify students too late for meaningful intervention. Multi-factor risk patterns spanning payment history, course engagement, and demographic proxies exist in the data but are never combined into a unified early-warning score.
ideas.ai_technique: Time-series classification (logistic regression + survival analysis)
ideas.how_it_works: The model combines enrollment demographics, course performance records, LMS login frequency, SMS engagement rates, and payment history — including partial payments and delays — into a risk score for each enrolled student. Trained on historical cohorts with known dropout outcomes, the model identifies timing windows: for example, finance students are most at risk in the two weeks after a payment deadline. The advisor dashboard shows the highest-risk students each week, their key risk drivers, and suggested interventions — fee relief referral, academic counselling, schedule change — matched to the risk profile.
ideas.scoring_rationale: Dropout is a high-severity problem at access-focused institutions where every lost student represents both revenue loss and mission failure. ideas.problem: PHINMA, iPeople, and Acasus evaluate expansion opportunities — new campus location, new program, new country — without rigorous market-sizing analysis. ideas.ai_technique: Regression with demographic and economic predictors

A regression model trained on comparable existing markets where the organisation has enrollment data estimates the addressable population and likely conversion rate. Sensitivity analysis shows how the estimate changes under different economic scenarios.
ideas.scoring_rationale: VPs of Strategy have budget authority and the use case is clear, but market sizing exercises happen once per expansion decision rather than continuously, which weakens the recurring SaaS revenue argument. ideas.name: Board Communication Workbench
ideas.one_liner: A single workspace to draft, compliance-check, route for approval, and archive trustee communications — replacing email threads and shared-drive folders as the de facto governance communication record.
ideas.problem: School for Life, Acasus, and WISE communicate with multiple governance bodies — boards, diocesan authorities, government funders — through email and shared drives. There is no audit-ready record of what was communicated, to whom, when, or whether it was approved before release. Governance auditors and accreditation reviewers frequently request communication histories that can't be reconstructed cleanly.
ideas.how_it_works: User drafts a governance communication — board memo, trustee letter, government compliance report — in the workbench. System classifies the content type, flags compliance risks (e.g., disclosure of sensitive accreditation status without board approval), routes to named approvers based on content classification, and archives the approved version with full metadata (author, approvers, distribution list, date). Integrates with Diligent or BoardEffect for distribution. Provides a searchable, tamper-evident archive for governance audits.
ideas.ai_technique: Document classification with compliance flag detection and role-based access control
ideas.scoring_rationale: Governance document workflow is well-served by existing tools (Diligent, BoardEffect, DocuSign) and the AI angle — compliance flagging — is thin. Low agentic suitability and greenfield advantage make this a marginal extension of BoardBrief rather than a standalone product.

ideas.name: Teacher Onboarding Curriculum Builder
ideas.one_liner: Generates a personalized onboarding plan for each new teacher — varying the depth, resources, and milestone schedule based on their experience level, subject area, and the campus they are joining.
ideas.problem: New teachers at Rising Academies and PHINMA receive the same generic onboarding regardless of whether they are a 10-year experienced hire or a graduate joining their first classroom. The experienced hire wastes days on basic orientation; the graduate gets no structured mentoring in the critical first 8 weeks when early attrition risk is highest. Inconsistency across 80+ campuses means onboarding quality depends on which administrator happens to be available.
ideas.ai_technique: Data Transformation & Structuring with personalization logic
ideas.how_it_works: Ingests the incoming teacher's profile (years of experience, subject, prior school type, role level) and the campus context (curriculum stage, accreditation status, enrollment trend, available mentor teachers). Selects from a library of onboarding modules — curriculum guides, NESA standard mapping, parent communication templates, pedagogical frameworks — and assembles a sequenced plan with week-by-week milestones. Graduate teachers receive an 8-week structured program with peer mentoring assigned by week 1; experienced hires get a 2-week targeted integration. Progress is tracked and the system flags a new hire as at-risk if key milestones slip past their target date.
ideas.buyer_persona: Onboarding Coordinator or Head of New Teacher Support
ideas.scoring_rationale: Low buyer clarity — 'Onboarding Coordinator' rarely has budget authority, and this problem is easy to dismiss as 'just use a checklist.' Build is feasible but the output is a document, not a system, making it hard to price as SaaS. Better positioned as a onboarding wizard feature inside a broader Staffroom HR product than a standalone offering.

ideas.name: Succession Readiness Planner
ideas.one_liner: Assesses internal leadership candidates against education-sector executive competency frameworks, identifies capability gaps, and generates a 12-month development plan tied to a succession timeline.
ideas.problem: Acasus, School for Life, and WISE are founder-led or dependent on long-serving CEOs who are approaching transitions. There is no structured process to identify and develop internal successors — capability assessments are informal, development plans are generic, and boards have no systematic visibility into succession risk until a departure is imminent.
ideas.how_it_works: System ingests 360-degree feedback data, documented contributions to board decisions, and education-sector executive competency frameworks (built for K-12 principal pipelines and university president roles). Generates a capability gap analysis comparing each candidate to the target role requirements, with specific gaps identified (e.g., board-level governance communication, cross-jurisdictional regulatory navigation). Auto-generates a 12-month development plan — board committee rotations, targeted coaching engagements, external education leadership programmes — with milestones tied to the succession timeline. Progress is reviewed quarterly with updated gap analysis.
ideas.ai_technique: Competency assessment combining structured feedback data, role-specific capability frameworks, and education-sector leadership benchmarks
ideas.buyer_persona: Board Chair, CEO, Chief Human Resources Officer
ideas.scoring_rationale: Succession planning for education executives is episodic and the 360-degree feedback data required rarely exists in structured form. Low data access and time to value scores reflect the setup cost before any output is useful. Adjacent to Staffroom's workforce intelligence positioning.

ideas.name: Partnership Opportunity Matcher
ideas.one_liner: Identifies credible curriculum, technology, and geographic expansion partners by indexing the sector landscape and matching against the institution's documented capability gaps.
ideas.problem: iPeople, PHINMA, and School for Life pursue partnerships for curriculum credibility, technology scaling, and geographic reach, but discovery is entirely network-dependent. Organisations miss complementary partners operating in adjacent geographies or verticals simply because no-one in the leadership team knows them. Manual due diligence is slow and inconsistent. Strategic partnership decisions are made on familiarity rather than systematic fit analysis.
ideas.ai_technique: Semantic similarity matching + market gap analysis
ideas.how_it_works: A partner index is built from public sources: edtech vendor capability pages, international university partnership programme listings, training organisation accreditation records, and employer partnership case studies. Each potential partner is profiled on capabilities, geographies served, target student segments, and pricing tier. The institution's own profile is defined by the strategy team: programs, geographies, technology gaps, and expansion targets. The tool runs similarity matching to surface high-fit candidates, assesses mutual complementarity — 'This partner operates in 3 of your 5 target expansion countries' — and generates a prioritised partnership pipeline with a pre-populated due-diligence checklist for each candidate.
ideas.buyer_persona: VP of Strategy / Academic Partnerships Director
ideas.scoring_rationale: Partnership discovery is a genuine gap but not an acute daily pain — organisations form a handful of strategic partnerships per year, making the recurring value of a dedicated SaaS tool hard to justify. Data access is the hardest constraint: there is no reliable structured index of education partnership opportunities to crawl.

ideas.name: Precedent Search Assistant
ideas.one_liner: Semantic search across an organisation's internal program evaluations, board papers, and pilot reports so strategy teams surface relevant past decisions in seconds rather than digging through shared drives.
ideas.problem: Strategy leaders at Kinetic and iPeople regularly revisit expansion decisions, program designs, and funding structures — but institutional memory lives in unindexed PDFs across Google Drive and SharePoint. Keyword search misses conceptually similar content ('reduce dropout rate' doesn't match a search for 'improve student retention'), so teams recreate analysis already done or miss hard-won lessons from failed pilots.
ideas.how_it_works: System ingests PDFs of program evaluations, feasibility studies, and board papers into a vector database with multi-lingual support. When a strategy leader queries ('What happened when we expanded to a new region?'), the system returns semantically ranked past pilots, their outcomes, decisions made, and enabling factors. Results link to source documents. Connects to existing Google Drive or SharePoint via OAuth.
ideas.ai_technique: Dense vector embeddings of education program reports with multi-lingual support for LMIC contexts
ideas.scoring_rationale: This is a standard enterprise RAG search tool applied to education documents — well-trodden territory with low defensibility and weak buyer clarity ('Strategy Manager' rarely owns SaaS budgets). Scores low on agentic suitability; it is fundamentally a retrieval tool with no multi-step reasoning requirement.

ideas.name: Governance Classifier
ideas.one_liner: Automatically routes incoming board submissions, audit findings, and policy notices to the correct oversight body — diocesan, government regulator, accreditation board, or funder committee — based on document type and escalation threshold.
ideas.problem: In multi-stakeholder school networks like PHINMA and CSPD, governance documents arrive from a mix of sources — Catholic diocesan offices, CHED, NESA, accreditation panels, investor committees — each requiring a different approval pathway. Board secretaries manually decide where each document goes, creating routing delays and compliance gaps when documents sit in the wrong queue.
ideas.how_it_works: Ingests documents submitted to the governance inbox (board papers, audit reports, policy change notices, accreditation correspondence). Applies a multi-label classifier to identify regulatory domain (Catholic governance, government compliance, accreditation evidence, financial reporting) and urgency. Outputs a routing decision with confidence score, assigns the document to the correct committee queue, and flags anomalies — e.g., a document with accreditation implications that was submitted only to the finance committee.
ideas.ai_technique: Text classification with multi-label hierarchical routing
ideas.buyer_persona: Governance Officer / Compliance Manager / Board Secretary
ideas.scoring_rationale: Document routing is a real pain in complex multi-regulator organisations, but the problem is solved intermittently rather than daily — routing failures are annoying, not catastrophic. The concept is nearly identical to Governance Document Classifier (idea 77) in this same batch, limiting standalone viability.
